title: Syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone secretion (SIADH)
body_html:
Definition
Syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone (SIADH) secretion is defined as euvolaemic, hypotonic hyponatraemia secondary to impaired free water excretion typically as a result of excessive antidiuretic hormone secretion.

Excess ADH may be secreted from the posterior pituitary gland or an abnormal non-pituitary source.

Diagnostic features:
  • Low plasma sodium concentration (<135 mmol/l)
  • Low serum osmolality (<275 mOsm/kg)
  • High urine osmolality (>100 mOsm/kg & greater than plasma osmolarity)
  • Euvolaemia
  • High urine sodium (>30 mmol/L)
  • No other cause for hyponatraemia (diuretic use, hypothyroidism, cortisol deficiency, hyperproteinaemia, hyperlipidaemia or hyperglycaemia).

Antidiuretic hormone (ADH) is also known as arginine vasopressin (AVP). It is synthesised in the hypothalamus and secreted from the posterior pituitary. It promotes water and sodium resorption.

As the name suggests the primary action of antidiuretic hormone is to prevent diuresis. It achieves this by increasing sodium and water resorption. Click on image to enlarge

Causes of SIADH
CategoryExamples
Malignancy
  • Small cell lung cancer
  • Stomach cancer
  • Oropharyngeal cancer
  • Pancreatic cancer
  • Prostate cancer
  • Leukaemia
  • Lymphoma
  • Thymoma
  • Genitourinary tract cancers
Neurological
  • Stroke
  • Subarachnoid haemorrhage
  • Subdural haemorrhage
  • Meningitis/encephalitis/abscess
  • Tumour
  • Guillain-Barre syndrome
  • Multiple sclerosis
Infections
  • Tuberculosis
  • Pneumonia
Drugs
  • Thiazide diuretics
  • SSRI's
  • Proton pump inhibitors
  • ACE inhibitors
  • Loop diuretics
  • Tricyclics
  • Sulfonylureas*
  • Carbamazepine
  • Vincristine
  • Cyclophosphamide
Respiratory
  • Asthma
  • Mesothelioma
  • Cystic fibrosis
  • Positive end-expiratory pressure (PEEP)
Other causes
  • Systemic lupus erythematosus
  • Porphyrias

*the BNF states this has been reported with glimepiride and glipizide.

Diagnostic features summary
  • Hyponatraemia.
  • Low plasma osmolality
  • Inappropriately elevated urine osmolality (>100 mOsmol/kg commonly and > plasma osmolarity)
  • Urinary sodium >30 mmol/L
  • Euvolaemia.
  • Normal thyroid and adrenal function.

Signs & symptoms of hyponatraemia
  • Nausea and vomiting.
  • Headache.
  • Confusion.
  • Lethargy and fatigue.
  • Restlessness and irritability.
  • Muscle weakness, spasms or cramps.
  • Drowsniess
  • Seizures.
  • Coma.

Management
  • Correct hyponatreamia slowly to avoid precipitating central pontine myelinolysis
  • Treat underlying cause where possible e.g. stop causative drugs
  • Fluid restriction (typically 1000 ml/day for adults)
  • Intravenous hypertonic saline (3% sodium chloride solution) is typically used in patients with neurological symptoms.
  • Demeclocycline: blocks ADH receptors & reduces responsiveness of the collecting tubule cells to ADH
  • ADH receptor antagonists (AKA vaptans or vasopressin receptor antagonists) such as tolvaptan are an option but have limited availability

Central pontine myelinolysis (osmotic demyelination syndrome) may occur with rapid correction of serum sodium in excess of 12 mmol/L/day.

If using hypertonic saline the serum sodium levels must be checked every 2 hours. The goal is to increase serum sodium slowly by up to 1 mmol/L/hour to a maximum of 10-12 mmol/L/day until neurological symptoms resolve.

Osmolality abnormalities & underlying causes

Serum osmolalityUrine osmolalityCauses
Normal or increasedIncreasedDehydration
Renal disease and uraemia
Congestive heart failure
Addison's disease
Hypercalcaemia
Diabetes mellitus/hyperglycaemia
Hypernatraemia
Alcohol ingestion
Mannitol therapy
Normal or increasedDecreasedDiabetes insipidus
DecreasedIncreasedSIADH
DecreasedDecreased
(with no increase in fluid intake)		Overhydration
Hyponatraemia
Adrenocortical insufficiency
Sodium loss (diuretic or a low-salt diet)
NCBI00
Endocrine Testing for the Syndrome of Inappropriate Antidiuretic Hormone Secretion (SIADH)
